title: Energy Budgeting

content.statement: Schedule tasks by energy, not time.
content.question: Are you protecting your peak-energy hours from low-value work?
content.business_lens: Creative in peak; email in low.
sop: Track your energy level hourly for 3-5 days (see calculation, page 5)
Sort your recurring tasks into high-focus (creative, strategic) and low-focus (admin, email) buckets
Assign high-focus tasks to your top 2 energy hours and low-focus tasks to your dip
Block the peak hours on your calendar as unmovable, labeled by task type, not just 'busy'
Review weekly and adjust the map as your schedule or season changes
content.explainer: Two hours of work aren't interchangeable just because they're both 'an hour.' The same task takes less effort and produces a better result when it's matched to a high-energy window, and more effort for a worse result in a low one. Scheduling by energy instead of by the clock is why the same to-do list can feel effortless on one day and impossible on another.
questions: Are you protecting your peak-energy hours from low-value work?
What's the one task you keep scheduling for your peak hours that doesn't actually need your best energy?
If you mapped your energy by hour for a week, would your calendar match the map, or fight it?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Energy 8, Efficiency 6. Output = 8 times 6 = 48. Higher score means you're doing high-leverage work while genuinely capable of it; lower means you're either burnt out or coasting through hours that could produce more.
content.closing_statement: Match the task to the energy, not the clock.

caption:
Principle Nº 208: Energy Budgeting

Schedule tasks by energy, not time. Two hours of work aren't interchangeable just because they're both 'an hour.' The same task takes less effort and produces a better result when it's matched to a high-energy window, and more effort for a worse result in a low one.

Ask yourself: Are you protecting your peak-energy hours from low-value work?
